findings.label: Does the deck include a clearly identified Problem slide?
Does the deck include a Solution slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 4, Slide 8, Slide 16

findings.label: Does the deck include a Team slide?
Does the deck include a Financials slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 12, Slide 14, Slide 18

findings.label: Does the deck include a Traction slide?
Does the deck include a Market slide?
Does the deck include a Cap Table or Funding slide?
Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
Does the deck have at least 8 slides?
Does the deck contain any specific revenue figures, ARR, MRR, or financial projections?

Does the deck provide a source, citation, or methodology for its TAM/SAM/SOM figures? A named research firm, report title, or calculation methodology counts. Vague phrases like 'industry data' do not count.
findings.evidence: ¹ Grand View Research: Global solar thermal market $32B (2025) + ² IEA Net Zero Scenario: High-insolation regions ~$6-8B subset + ³ GCC/MENA cumulative opportunity 2026-2030 + ⁴ Ceiling scenario assuming full mandate enforcement

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of customer discovery or validated demand — such as interviews, surveys, letters of intent, waitlists, or pilot results?
findings.suggestion: You've built a compelling regulatory and market case, but investors will want to know if actual builders and homeowners are asking for this solution—add a slide showing 3-5 specific customer conversations (even brief quotes like "We lose 40% of projects to reliability concerns" from a villa developer, or "maintenance costs are killing our margins" from a property manager) plus any letters of intent or pilot commitments you've secured, even if small.

findings.label: Does the deck clearly define the target customer segment with specific characteristics — not just broad demographics?
findings.evidence: UAE Villas & Developers — $400M Segment + Flat-roof architecture dominant → ideal fit for SolarisKit GCC + MENA Focus market

findings.label: Does the deck explain why this problem is urgent or why now is the right time to solve it?
findings.evidence: Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel), Rising electricity demand from water heating, Strong push for decarbonisation (e.g. UAE Climate Law), Large and growing pipeline of new villa developments
findings.suggestion: You've got the market drivers listed, but they're sitting at the bottom like supporting facts rather than the reason this moment is critical—add a timeline showing when these forces converge (e.g., "Estidama Phase 3 enforcement begins Q3 2025, coinciding with 40% YoY growth in villa starts, creating a 18-month window before legacy systems become non-compliant"). This transforms "tightening regulations" from background context into a specific urgency that explains why investors should care about funding you now, not next year.

findings.label: Does the deck address any regulatory or compliance factors relevant to the market?
Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: HelioHelp - AI Deployment Layer: Automated system design and sizing, AI-guided installation and troubleshooting, Performance and carbon modelling, Knowledge engine for global installers and partners. Proprietary design and performance modelling. In development — v1.0 targeted Q3 2026
findings.suggestion: Right now HelioHelp reads like a feature list—you need to show *how* the AI actually works on a real problem. Add one concrete example: walk through what happens when an installer in, say, Dubai inputs a villa's roof dimensions and hot water demand into HelioHelp, and show the specific output (e.g., "system recommends 8 collectors, predicts 12,000 kWh annual savings, flags hard-water risk requiring polymer treatment"). This transforms it from "we have AI" to "here's the intelligence that lets us scale without hiring 50 engineers."

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: Patented collector technology (US, EU, UK); Purpose-built for flat-roof GCC architecture (structural niche); Hard-water polymer design → long-term reliability advantage; Flat-pack manufacturing → cost and logistics advantage; Proprietary design and performance modelling; Installer knowledge platform; Deployment data and system optimisation; As deployments grow → data, installer network, and platform value increase → defensibility strengthens.

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: Full retrofit: 570 solar collector system deployment (underway). + Delivery underway — Q2 2026

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: Land → Expand → Scale model. Hotels, utilities, and developers validate reliability and ROI. Projects convert into repeat deployments through developer frameworks and retrofit programmes. Utility and ESCO partners deploy across large building portfolios.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.suggestion: You've built HelioHelp as a competitive advantage, but investors will want to know what's under the hood—specifically, which cloud platform hosts it, what LLM or AI framework powers the "proprietary AI engine," and whether you're dependent on third-party APIs for weather data, building permits, or utility integration. Add a single sentence to Slide 19 like "HelioHelp runs on AWS with a custom ML model trained on 50K+ global installations, using OpenWeather API for location-based sizing" so investors understand your tech stack isn't a black box and can assess scaling costs and vendor lock-in risk themselves.

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.suggestion: You're operating in a regulated market (UAE mandates solar hot water, you're handling customer data through HelioHelp's AI design platform, and you're storing performance/carbon data from deployments), but investors won't see how you're managing compliance—add one slide in the appendix that covers data residency for HelioHelp (where customer system designs and performance data live), your approach to UAE/EU data regulations, and any certifications beyond ISO/EN (like data protection or cybersecurity standards relevant to building systems). For example: "HelioHelp data architecture: Customer designs and performance data stored in [region], compliant with [specific regulation], audited annually by [third party]

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?
findings.evidence: The slides present financial projections with scenarios (Bear, Base, Bull) and unit economics components, but do NOT explicitly state growth rate assumptions, customer acquisition assumptions, or detailed cost structure logic. The slides mention 'Conservative projections with base/conservative scenarios' and reference 'Unit Economics' with gross margins and deployment models, but lack explicit stated assumptions for how the projections were derived.
findings.suggestion: Add a single assumptions box to your financials slide that spells out the three drivers of your Base case: hardware unit volume growth (e.g., "50 units 2026 → 500 units 2028 based on Rixos + ALDAR pipeline conversion"), average selling price per unit (e.g., "$21K hardware ASP from current Rixos deal, scaling to $18K as manufacturing ramps"), and gross margin path (e.g., "35% 2026 rising to 45% by 2028 via localized injection molding"). This transforms your numbers from a black box into a credible story investors can stress-test and believe.

findings.label: Does the deck state the current burn rate and runway in months?
Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: Unit Economics: Hotels 38–46% and Villas 35–43%; Rixos Hotel ROI: 3.1 years
findings.suggestion: You're showing gross margins (35–46% for hotels, 38–45% overall) but investors need to see the full unit economics story—specifically your Customer Acquisition Cost, how long it takes to recover that cost, and your LTV:CAC ratio. Add a small table on Slide 14 showing: CAC per hotel/villa installation (e.g., $8K for developer partnerships), payback period in months (e.g., 14 months based on recurring O&M revenue), and LTV:CAC ratio (e.g., 3.2x over 5 years)—this transforms your margins into a compelling proof that your model compounds profitably.

findings.label: Are there any numbers that appear inconsistent across different slides — for example revenue figures that don't match between summary and detail slides?
findings.evidence: Slide 12 shows 'CONTRACTED & DEPLOYING - $213K (Q2 2026 delivery)' with total $213,000. Slide 14 shows 2026 Hardware Revenue as '$0.3M' which equals $300,000, creating a discrepancy between the $213K contracted amount and the $300K projected revenue.
findings.suggestion: On Slide 14, your 2026 hardware revenue shows $0.3M, but Slide 12 only has $213K in contracted deals—that's a $87K gap that investors will immediately spot and question your math on. Clarify whether the $0.3M includes pipeline deals beyond the two named customers (like the strategic discussions with Abu Dhabi Ports or ALDAR), and if so, add a footnote on Slide 14 breaking down the $0.3M as "$213K contracted + $87K from [specific pipeline stage/customer type]" so the progression feels earned, not assumed.

findings.label: Does the deck explain what the investment will be used for — with a specific breakdown of use of funds?
findings.suggestion: You're showing strong pipeline momentum and unit economics, but investors need to see exactly how you'll deploy this new capital to hit those 2026-2027 targets—add a dedicated use-of-funds slide that breaks down the $350K seed round into specific buckets like "manufacturing setup & inventory ($120K), sales & BD team ($100K), pilot deployment & installation ($80K), working capital ($50K)" tied directly to your contracted deals and hiring timeline. This removes the guesswork and shows you've thought through execution, not just the vision.

findings.label: Does the deck mention the path to profitability or breakeven point?
Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.evidence: Rixos (live project) and EtihadWE listed under revenue streams, but no explicit confirmation of payment or revenue received
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to the Rixos and EtihadWE bullets stating the payment status—something like "Rixos (live project, $X revenue received Q3 2024)" or "EtihadWE (contract signed, first payment received [date])." This transforms them from project names into proof points that money has actually changed hands, which is what investors need to see.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: MANUFACTURE MONETISE SCALE: UAE Factory Revenue Market Ras Al Khaimah Streams Routes PRODUCTION CAPACITY DIRECT SALES HOTELS / RESORTS / PROPERTY +24,000 units p.a. +$50-250K / Project DEVELOPERS UTILITIES LICENSING +6-12% royalties GLOBAL INSTALLATION PARTNERS +Regional rights +Rheem MEA (MOU signed) +Low capex scaling +Regional distributors

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.suggestion: Right now you're showing capacity and partnerships, but investors need to see actual traction—add specific numbers like "Generated $X revenue in [month], up Y% from [prior month]" or "Deployed 500 units in Q3 vs. 200 in Q2" so they can see momentum, not just potential. Even if you're early, showing the trajectory of what's actually happening (even if it's small) is way more compelling than the best partnership announcement.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.suggestion: You've got a killer proof point with Rixos staying operational through the December storm while competitors failed — now add one sentence to that timeline showing the customer renewed or expanded their commitment because of that reliability, or add a retention metric like "100% of pilot customers proceeding to full deployment" to prove this isn't a one-off win but a pattern investors can bet on.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?
Does the deck name specific competitors — not just generic categories like 'traditional players' or 'legacy solutions'?
findings.suggestion: Name the actual competitors in your GCC market instead of generic categories—investors want to know you've done the homework on who you're really up against. Replace "Legacy Solar" with something like "Solahart's flat-mount systems" or "Clyde's heat pump units" (or whoever the actual market leaders are), so investors can immediately picture the competitive set and understand why your specific design wins against real alternatives, not abstract ones.

findings.label: Does the competitive analysis appear superficial — for example a magic quadrant where the company leads in all dimensions without explanation?
findings.evidence: SolarisKit is marked with strong advantage (●) across nearly all dimensions: Flat-Roof Suitability, Power Consumption, Energy Savings, Hard Water Resistance, Overheating Protection, Maintenance Complexity, and Storm/Wind Resilience, while competitors are marked with weaknesses (○) or conditional marks (▲), with no explanation provided for why SolarisKit achieves superiority across all categories

findings.label: Does the deck address the threat of large incumbents or well-funded competitors entering the space?
findings.suggestion: Your comparison table shows why SolarisKit wins against existing solutions, but it doesn't address what happens when a major player like Bosch, Carrier, or a well-funded solar company decides to build exactly what you're building. Add a "Defensibility" section that explains your specific moats—for example: "SolarisKit's polymer absorber design is patent-pending (filed Q3 2024), our Gulf-storm testing data took 18 months to generate, and our flat-roof hydraulic architecture requires GCC-specific engineering that incumbents would need to rebuild from scratch." This transforms your comparison from "we're better today" to "we're structurally hard to copy."

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: To be recruited (post raise) and Recruited (post raise) appear in the organizational chart for Product Development Engineer and Installation Manager positions

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: Chartered mechanical engineer with 20 years of experience in solar thermal technology gained from industry, academia, and enterprise. Highly experienced in solar thermal technology, manufacturing, and quality. +Launched multiple products into the Australian solar thermal market generating >$10M AUD in revenue +Scotland's inventor of the year in 2021 +Previous Enterprise Fellow Royal Society of Edinburgh and winner of Net Zero Edge Prize, COP26, Glasgow

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.suggestion: You've got strong individual credentials, but you're missing a layer of credibility that investors lean on heavily—add 2-3 specific advisors or board members who bring either deep industry relationships (like a former utility executive or major solar distributor) or capital connections (like a successful solar thermal founder who's exited before). For example, you could add a line like "Advisory Board: John Smith, former VP of SunPower EMEA (led $200M+ in regional expansion)" right after your team table—this signals you've thought about de-risking execution and have people in your corner who've done this before.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.suggestion: Add a single line to your Team slide stating the vesting schedule for you and your co-founders—something like "Founder equity: 4-year vest with 1-year cliff" or whatever your actual terms are. Investors always ask this in the room, and having it visible shows you've thought like an owner and removes a question that could derail momentum during your pitch.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: Solar hot water is mandated across the UAE — yet adoption remains limited due to poor system reliability and climate mismatch. + Tightening building regulations (Estidama, Dubai, Barjeel)
findings.suggestion: You've got Solar Keymark certification and patents locked down, but investors in regulated markets will want to see explicit compliance with the specific building codes you mention—Estidama, Dubai, and Barjeel standards. Add a one-line callout on Slide 2 or 3 stating something like "Compliant with UAE building mandates: Estidama (Abu Dhabi), Dubai Municipality Code, Barjeel (RAK)" so it's crystal clear you've already cleared the regulatory hurdles, not just that they exist. This removes a due diligence blocker before it becomes a question.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.suggestion: You've got patents locked down and risk mitigations solid, but investors will want to see you've thought through the legal landscape more comprehensively—add a brief slide or section stating whether you've conducted freedom-to-operate (FTO) analysis on your core patents, confirmed no conflicting claims exist in your target markets (UAE, GCC, EU), and clarified your IP strategy for HelioHelp (since the trademark is still in application). This doesn't need to be lengthy—just one or two sentences per point showing you've done the homework, something like: "FTO analysis completed across US, EU, GB jurisdictions—no blocking patents identified in flat-roof solar thermal space; Helio

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.evidence: Slide 1 states '$207K Project Contract Value' while Slide 4 states 'PO Value: AED760K ($207K USD)' — these align. However, Slide 6 presents market sizing figures ($32B TAM, $400M SAM, $2B SOM segment) that cannot be verified against other slides for internal consistency, and the relationship between '5% UAE market share = $20M annual revenue opportunity' and the $400M SAM figure is unclear without additional context.
findings.suggestion: On Slide 6, add a single line showing how the 5% UAE market share ($20M) connects to your $400M SAM — for example: "5% UAE market share of $400M SAM = $20M annual revenue opportunity" or show the math explicitly so investors see the relationship isn't a standalone claim but grounded in your sizing logic. Right now it reads like two separate figures, which creates doubt even though the numbers likely work.

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?
findings.evidence: The world's first flat-packed solar collector

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.evidence: Multiple instances of incomplete text: 'Strong push for dec' (Slide 6 cuts off mid-word), '[URL_REDACTED]' placeholders appear throughout, duplicate slide content provided
findings.suggestion: Go back through the entire deck and do a final pass to catch every incomplete sentence, placeholder, and duplicate slide — you've got "Strong push for dec" cut off mid-word on Slide 6, [URL_REDACTED] appearing throughout instead of actual image credits or links, and Slide 1 plus several others duplicated in full. Pick one afternoon, open the master file, search for "[URL" and "dec" and fix line breaks, then delete every duplicate slide section so investors see one clean version of each. This takes 90 minutes and transforms how polished the deck feels.
